q: Why are zombie processes retained temporarily?
opts: To store exit status for the parent process
exp: A zombie process keeps its entry in the process table so the parent can read its exit status via wait(). Once the parent reads it, the zombie is removed.

q: What happens in a uniprocessing system when a process waits for I/O?
opts: The CPU executes all waiting processes
The CPU switches to kernel-only execution
The CPU may remain idle, wasting processing time
exp: In a uniprocessing system with no multiprogramming, the CPU sits idle while the process waits for I/O — wasting processing time.

q: A process that has completed execution but still exists in the process table is called a ___ process.
exp: A zombie process has finished execution but its entry remains in the process table until the parent process reads its exit status.

q: A process that is waiting for an event or a resource is said to be in the ___ state.
exp: A process enters the waiting (blocked) state when it cannot proceed until an event occurs, such as I/O completion or a signal.

q: Executing multiple threads within a single process improves performance through ___.
exp: Multithreading allows concurrent execution of tasks within a process, improving throughput through parallelism.

q: A process that has finished execution but remains in the process table is called a ___ process.
exp: Zombie processes linger in the process table after termination until the parent collects the exit status with wait().

q: How does the dispatcher differ from the scheduler?
opts: The dispatcher performs the actual context switch
exp: The scheduler decides which process runs next; the dispatcher actually performs the context switch and hands control of the CPU to the selected process.

q: IPC stands for ___.
exp: IPC (Inter-Process Communication) refers to mechanisms that allow processes to communicate and synchronize, such as pipes, message queues, and shared memory.

q: Multithreading improves performance through ___.
exp: Multithreading allows multiple threads to run concurrently, improving performance through parallelism especially on multi-core processors.

q: A ___ is the smallest unit of processing that can be scheduled by the operating system.
exp: A thread is the smallest schedulable unit. Multiple threads can exist within a single process, sharing its memory space.

q: Which scenario best represents multiprocessing?
opts: Multiple CPUs executing different processes simultaneously
Context switching on a single CPU
Threads sharing memory within one process
exp: Multiprocessing uses multiple CPUs to execute different processes simultaneously, providing true parallelism — unlike multitasking which time-shares a single CPU.

q: The act of reassigning the CPU from one task to another is called a ___.
exp: A context switch saves the state of the current process and loads the state of the next process, allowing the CPU to switch between tasks.

q: Which condition defines an uninterruptible waiting process?
opts: Waiting directly on a hardware condition
exp: An uninterruptible wait (D state in Linux) occurs when a process is waiting directly on a hardware condition and cannot be interrupted by signals.

q: The component that performs the actual CPU allocation and context switching is the ___.
exp: The dispatcher handles the mechanics of the context switch — saving/restoring registers, switching memory maps, and handing CPU control to the next process.

q: Which scenario best describes a context switch?
opts: Saving one process state and loading another onto the CPU
exp: A context switch saves the CPU state (registers, program counter) of the current process and restores the state of the next process to be scheduled.

q: Why is the CPU considered the most precious resource in a Linux system?
opts: The CPU only runs kernel processes
The CPU controls all memory directly
The number of processes usually exceeds the number of CPUs
exp: There are always more processes wanting to run than there are CPUs available, making CPU time scarce and requiring careful scheduling.

q: The Linux component responsible for choosing the next process to run is the ___.
exp: The Linux scheduler (e.g., CFS — Completely Fair Scheduler) decides which process gets the CPU next based on priority and fairness policies.

q: Which statement best distinguishes a process from a program in Linux?
opts: A process is an active execution of a program with allocated system resources
Multiple programs can belong to one process
A program consumes CPU resources, while a process does not
A process is a passive entity stored on disk
A program is an executing instance of a process
exp: A program is a passive set of instructions on disk. A process is an active instance of a program with its own memory, CPU registers, and resources.

q: Which process component ensures correct resumption after a context switch?
exp: The program counter and CPU registers are saved during a context switch and restored when the process resumes, ensuring execution continues from the right point.

q: Why does Linux treat processes as fundamental units of operation?
opts: All system work requires kernel-managed resources allocated to processes
exp: Every piece of work in Linux requires kernel-managed resources (CPU time, memory, file handles) — all of which are allocated per process, making processes the fundamental unit.

q: A process waiting for an event or resource is in the ___ state.
exp: A process in the waiting/blocked state is not using the CPU — it is paused until a resource becomes available or an event (like I/O) completes.

q: What is the primary role of the Linux scheduler?
opts: Deciding which process should run next
exp: The scheduler's job is to decide which process runs next. The actual mechanics of switching (context switch) are handled by the dispatcher.

q: A process waiting for an event that can be interrupted by signals is in an ___ waiting state.
exp: An interruptible wait (S state in Linux) means the process can be woken by a signal. An uninterruptible wait (D state) cannot be interrupted.

q: Time-sharing provides the illusion that processes are executing ___.
exp: Time-sharing rapidly switches the CPU between processes, giving each a short time slice so they all appear to run at the same time to the user.

q: What is the primary purpose of time-sharing in Linux?
opts: To give the illusion that multiple processes execute simultaneously
exp: Time-sharing rapidly switches the CPU between processes so that to users and applications, all processes appear to run concurrently.

q: What is the main benefit of separate virtual address spaces for processes?
exp: Separate virtual address spaces mean one process cannot corrupt another's memory. A crash or bug in one process doesn't bring down the whole system.

q: Which process state indicates that processes are competing for CPU time?
exp: The Ready state means a process is loaded in memory and waiting for CPU time. Multiple ready processes compete to be scheduled next.
